role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3011 Advertising Sales Agents 8.2% of wage bill + 41-3091 Sales Reps of Services 3.0% — note the role_mix is 4-digit 5418, dominated by ad agencies (541810); for actual OOH operators sales is the core commercial function
role_hints.note: Prospecting, proposal decks, rate-card quoting and availability lookups automate substantially; local advertiser relationships and programmatic-platform negotiations persist. Programmatic DOOH is already shifting the transaction to platforms, which is a distribution question, not an automation one.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General & Operations Managers 9.3% + 11-2021 Marketing Managers 5.2%; site-lease acquisition and permit management staff are not in the listed top-10 (long tail, est. ~5-10% for actual OOH firms)
role_hints.note: Lease renewals, landowner relations and permit fights are relationship/legal work that persists; internal reporting, billing and inventory management automate.

role_hints.role: Marketing, research and PR-type staff (mostly 5418 contamination)
role_hints.oews_basis: 27-3031 Public Relations Specialists 8.3% + 13-1161 Market Research Analysts 6.6% — these shares reflect ad agencies and PR firms inside 4-digit 5418, not billboard operators; treat as overstated for 541850
role_hints.approx_share_of_wage_bill: ~5% in a real OOH firm (record proxy says ~15%)
role_hints.note: Audience measurement packaging (impressions, Geopath data), campaign reporting and proof-of-performance decks are near-fully automatable where they exist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 27-1024 Graphic Designers 3.3% + 27-1011 Art Directors 4.1% (again 5418-agency-weighted; OOH operators carry a thinner version)
role_hints.note: Billboard/display creative and copy layout is among the most directly AI-automatable work in the firm; many operators already outsource it.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in the listed top-10 — billposters, sign installers (47-xxxx/49-xxxx), large-format print operators and vehicle crews; long tail, est. ~25-35% for actual display-advertising operators
role_hints.note: Physically hanging vinyls, servicing digital boards at height, and maintaining structures cannot be automated by current AI; this is the largest AI-resistant block in a real OOH firm and the record's agency-flavored proxy hides it.

role_hints.oews_basis: long tail of office/admin occupations not in the listed top-10, est. remainder ~15-20%
role_hints.note: Billing, contract admin, scheduling of postings and collections automate near-fully.

source_hints.source: OAAA (Out of Home Advertising Association of America) industry revenue and technology reports
source_hints.why: The OOH trade association; publishes revenue, digital-board penetration and (recently) AI/programmatic adoption commentary usable for current_adoption_pct.
Shows how far automated audience measurement has penetrated OOH inventory — a concrete adoption indicator.

source_hints.source: Vistar Media / Place Exchange programmatic DOOH market commentary
source_hints.why: Evidence on what share of OOH transactions run through programmatic platforms and what take-rate the platforms extract — central to pi_dist.

source_hints.source: Billboard Insider (trade publication) valuation and transaction commentary
source_hints.why: Covers independent billboard operator sales and routinely discusses cash-flow multiples for plant sales — the best public window on small-operator buy multiples.

source_hints.source: Lamar Advertising, OUTFRONT Media, Clear Channel Outdoor public filings (10-K, acquisition disclosures)
source_hints.why: Exit-multiple anchors and documented tuck-in acquisition activity by the big three consolidators.

source_hints.source: OAAA membership / independent operator profiles and Billboard Insider operator interviews
source_hints.why: Independent billboard plants are frequently multi-generation family businesses; succession evidence is anecdotal but consistent — verify rather than assume.

capture_questions: OOH space is sold off rate cards priced on location scarcity and audience impressions, not on the operator's cost base — when AI cuts sales, design and back-office labor, does the operator keep the savings as margin (location rents are set by demand), or does programmatic DOOH price transparency compress rates toward marginal cost?

Programmatic DOOH platforms (Vistar, Place Exchange, Broadsign) increasingly own the advertiser transaction — what take-rate do they extract, and does the independent operator become a passive inventory landlord with the AI-era margin accruing to the platform?

Who owns the two relationships that matter — the landowner lease (typically firm-held, long-term, transferable) and the local advertiser account (often personal to the owner-operator) — and what fraction of revenue survives the founder's exit?

Can advertisers bypass the operator with generic AI at all, given that the scarce asset is a permitted physical location rather than a service — i.e., is the AI-bypass threat effectively zero here, and should pi_moat reflect asset scarcity rather than service defensibility?
terminal_value_question: The billboard operator's product is a permitted physical location — a zoning-scarce real asset AI cannot replicate — so mature AI cuts this operator's costs rather than replacing its product; the real terminal question is whether advertiser demand for OOH holds as AI-optimized digital channels compete for the same budgets, and whether permit scarcity (many jurisdictions cap or ban new billboards, grandfathering existing ones) keeps location rents intact. Which is it: cost-cutter on a protected asset, or slow demand melt of the medium itself?
special_notes: Role-mix contamination: the record's role_mix is 4-digit 5418 and is dominated by ad-agency/PR occupations (PR specialists 8.3%, market research analysts 6.6% of wage bill) that barely exist inside a billboard/display operator. Real 541850 firms are sales + lease management + physical installation/print crews. Re-weight before setting within-role fractions.

This is an asset business wearing a services NAICS code: value sits in permitted structures and site leases, not labor. Labor share is only 0.3265 and the n_band derivation applies a generic 15.65% Business & Consumer Services margin — actual billboard operators run far higher EBITDA margins (often 30-50% on mature plants), so the record's n_band (112) likely undercounts band-eligible firms. Note this when scoring N.

Multiples quirk: billboard plants trade on cash-flow multiples closer to real-estate/infrastructure than to services (Lamar and OUTFRONT are REITs). Do not import professional-services multiples; use plant-sale comps (Billboard Insider, public tuck-in disclosures).

Boundary confusion: ad agencies are 541810, media buying is 541830, media reps are 541840, direct mail is 541860, sign manufacturing is 339950, and transit-ad operators sometimes classify under 541850 or 541890 — association revenue figures (OAAA) span the whole OOH medium including the big-three public companies; strip them before reasoning about the independent-operator segment.

Size structure: 1,543 of 2,083 firms are <5 employees (tiny local plants), and the big three control a large share of national inventory — the roll-up universe is the middle (20-99 employee class, 84 firms) plus sub-scale plants valued on cash flow rather than EBITDA-band logic.
Consolidation is old news here — Lamar/OUTFRONT/Clear Channel have been tucking in independents for decades. Usable for B and M only; never as evidence for V or A (rule 2).
